content.text: Training a robot policy in the real world is prohibitively slow and dangerous. A manipulator arm takes tens of thousands of trials to learn a grasping strategy; a legged robot crashes repeatedly before walking. Physics simulators solve this by providing a fast, safe, and infinitely resetable environment where an agent can accumulate experience orders of magnitude faster than on physical hardware. The quality of that simulated experience — how accurately the virtual world models contact forces, inertia, and sensor noise — determines whether the learned policy will transfer to the real robot.

is software that numerically integrates the equations of motion for a collection of rigid bodies, contacts, and actuators over discrete time steps. Given initial positions, velocities, masses, and joint configurations, the simulator advances the world state by solving Newton's laws subject to constraint forces at contacts and joints. The result is a sequence of states that closely approximate — but never perfectly replicate — what real hardware would do.
The major simulators used in robot learning today are
(Python interface to the Bullet physics engine),
(Multi-Joint dynamics with Contact, now free from DeepMind),
(the standard ROS2 simulation environment). Each makes different tradeoffs between physical accuracy, simulation speed, rendering quality, and ease of use.

Reinforcement learning algorithms for robotics require millions of environment interactions to converge. At one interaction per second on real hardware, one million steps takes eleven days. A well-tuned simulator running on a modern CPU can step the same environment at 10,000–100,000 Hz, compressing that training time to minutes or hours. This is not merely a convenience — it is what makes deep RL for robotics tractable at all.

Beyond speed, simulators provide safety guarantees that real hardware cannot. An algorithm exploring a new policy can crash the simulated robot 10,000 times without damaging anything. Simulators also allow precise control over the environment state — resetting to an exact initial condition, injecting faults, scanning a grid of parameter values — capabilities that are impossible or extremely expensive on real systems.
Simulation can run 10x–10,000x faster than real time, enabling millions of environment interactions that would take days on hardware.
Policies can explore aggressively and fail repeatedly without risk of hardware damage or human injury.
Exact environment states can be saved and restored. Experiments are reproducible given the same seed, which is impossible on real hardware.
Multiple simulation instances can run in parallel on one machine, providing near-linear speedup in data collection throughput.
Physics parameters (mass, friction, stiffness) can be varied programmatically across episodes, a core technique for sim-to-real transfer.
Cameras, depth sensors, force-torque sensors, and IMUs can be added and configured freely without hardware procurement.

: the difference between simulated and real physics. Contact dynamics are particularly hard to model accurately. Friction is famously difficult — its value depends on surface temperature, wear, and normal force in ways that rigid-body models ignore. Actuator dynamics (motor inertia, gear backlash, PID controller lag) are simplified or omitted entirely in many simulators.
Contact, deformable materials, and fluid dynamics are approximate. Policies that exploit simulation artifacts will fail on real hardware.
Even photorealistic renderers differ from real camera images in lighting, material reflectance, and sensor noise characteristics.
Building a high-fidelity robot model (URDF, SDF, USD) with accurate mass/inertia parameters and collision meshes is time-consuming.
High-fidelity simulators with accurate contact solvers are slower than simplified ones. The best simulator for RL is often not the most physically accurate.
GPU-based simulators like Isaac Sim require high-end NVIDIA hardware. Running 1000 parallel environments demands significant memory bandwidth.
At each simulation time step (typically 1–10 ms), the physics engine performs three main operations. First,
uses bounding volume hierarchies or spatial hashing to quickly identify pairs of bodies that might be touching. Second,
computes the exact contact points, normals, and penetration depths between colliding pairs. Third, the

computes joint and contact constraint forces (using methods like projected Gauss-Seidel or direct LCP solvers), and finally integrates the equations of motion forward using a semi-implicit Euler or Runge-Kutta scheme.

MuJoCo uses generalized coordinates (joint angles rather than Cartesian positions) and a Gauss-based physics formulation that handles contacts and constraints in a unified differentiable framework. This makes it exceptionally accurate for contact-rich manipulation tasks. PyBullet uses the Bullet physics engine's iterative constraint solver, which is faster but less accurate for stiff contacts. Isaac Sim wraps NVIDIA's PhysX engine with GPU compute kernels, enabling thousands of parallel environments on a single A100 GPU.

attrs.alt: Physics simulators architecture: PyBullet, MuJoCo, Isaac Sim, and Gazebo each connect to a shared Physics Engine Core layer covering rigid body dynamics, collision detection, contact simulation, and joint constraints. The core feeds into an RL Training Loop and a ROS2 Integration block.

content.text: The diagram shows how each simulator wraps the same fundamental physics primitives. PyBullet and Gazebo are best suited for rapid prototyping and ROS2 integration respectively. MuJoCo dominates contact-rich manipulation research due to its accurate constraint solver. Isaac Sim is the choice when photorealistic rendering and massive parallel training are required.
for rapid RL prototyping where Gymnasium compatibility matters and you want a free, Python-native setup with minimal configuration.
for dexterous manipulation, locomotion, and any task with complex contact dynamics. It is the de-facto standard in academic robot learning.
when you need thousands of parallel environments on GPU, photorealistic perception data, or tight integration with NVIDIA's Omniverse ecosystem for domain randomization.
when ROS2 integration is a hard requirement, when deploying multi-robot scenarios, or when you need plugin-based sensor modeling (LIDAR, GPS, IMU) in a standard way.
The standard workflow for training a robot policy in simulation follows three phases. In the
, you load the robot URDF/SDF file, configure joint limits, mass/inertia parameters, and contact surfaces. In the
, a Gymnasium-compatible environment wraps the simulator, exposing
interfaces that an RL algorithm can consume. In the
, the trained policy is exported (typically as a TorchScript or ONNX model) and loaded on the real robot's onboard computer.

# Example 1: Training a robot arm to reach a target in PyBullet
# pip install pybullet gymnasium stable-baselines3

import pybullet as p
import pybullet_data
import numpy as np
import gymnasium as gym
from gymnasium import spaces


class RobotArmReachEnv(gym.Env):
    """A simple reach task: move the end-effector to a target position."""

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.physics_client = p.connect(p.DIRECT)  # headless; use p.GUI for visual
        p.setAdditionalSearchPath(pybullet_data.getDataPath())
        p.setGravity(0, 0, -9.81)

        # Action: target joint velocities for 6-DOF arm, normalized [-1, 1]
        self.action_space = spaces.Box(low=-1.0, high=1.0, shape=(6,), dtype=np.float32)
        # Observation: joint angles (6) + end-effector xyz (3) + target xyz (3)
        self.observation_space = spaces.Box(
            low=-np.pi, high=np.pi, shape=(12,), dtype=np.float32
        )

        self._load_robot()
        self.target_pos = np.array([0.5, 0.0, 0.3])

    def _load_robot(self):
        """Load the Kuka IIWA from PyBullet's data directory."""
        p.loadURDF("plane.urdf")
        self.robot = p.loadURDF(
            "kuka_iiwa/model.urdf",
            basePosition=[0, 0, 0],
            useFixedBase=True,
        )
        self.num_joints = p.getNumJoints(self.robot)
        self.joint_indices = [
            i for i in range(self.num_joints)
            if p.getJointInfo(self.robot, i)[2] != p.JOINT_FIXED
        ]

    def _get_obs(self):
        joint_states = p.getJointStates(self.robot, self.joint_indices)
        joint_angles = np.array([s[0] for s in joint_states], dtype=np.float32)
        ee_link = self.joint_indices[-1]
        ee_pos = np.array(p.getLinkState(self.robot, ee_link)[0], dtype=np.float32)
        return np.concatenate([joint_angles, ee_pos, self.target_pos.astype(np.float32)])

    def step(self, action):
        # Scale actions to joint velocity range
        velocities = action * 0.5  # max 0.5 rad/s
        p.setJointMotorControlArray(
            self.robot,
            self.joint_indices,
            p.VELOCITY_CONTROL,
            targetVelocities=velocities,
        )
        p.stepSimulation()

        obs = self._get_obs()
        ee_pos = obs[6:9]
        dist = np.linalg.norm(ee_pos - self.target_pos)

        reward = -dist  # dense reward: negative distance
        terminated = dist < 0.02  # success threshold 2 cm
        truncated = False
        return obs, float(reward), terminated, truncated, {"dist": dist}

    def reset(self, seed=None, options=None):
        super().reset(seed=seed)
        # Reset all joints to zero
        for i, idx in enumerate(self.joint_indices):
            p.resetJointState(self.robot, idx, targetValue=0.0)
        # Randomize target position in front of the robot
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
        self.target_pos = rng.uniform([0.3, -0.3, 0.2], [0.7, 0.3, 0.6])
        return self._get_obs(), {}

    def close(self):
        p.disconnect(self.physics_client)

# Example 2: Training the reach policy with Stable-Baselines3
# Continues from the environment above

from stable_baselines3 import SAC
from stable_baselines3.common.env_util import make_vec_env
from stable_baselines3.common.callbacks import EvalCallback
import gymnasium as gym

# Vectorize 4 environments for parallel data collection
env = make_vec_env(RobotArmReachEnv, n_envs=4)
eval_env = RobotArmReachEnv()

# SAC is sample-efficient for continuous control
model = SAC(
    "MlpPolicy",
    env,
    verbose=1,
    learning_rate=3e-4,
    buffer_size=500_000,
    batch_size=256,
    ent_coef="auto",     # automatic entropy tuning
    tensorboard_log="./tb_logs/reach/",
)

# Evaluate every 10k steps; save best model
eval_callback = EvalCallback(
    eval_env,
    best_model_save_path="./models/reach_best/",
    eval_freq=10_000,
    n_eval_episodes=10,
    deterministic=True,
)

# Train for 500k steps (takes ~5 min on a modern CPU with 4 envs)
model.learn(total_timesteps=500_000, callback=eval_callback)
model.save("reach_final")

# Load and run inference
model = SAC.load("reach_final")
obs, _ = eval_env.reset()
for _ in range(200):
    action, _ = model.predict(obs, deterministic=True)
    obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info = eval_env.step(action)
    if terminated:
        print(f"Reached target! Final dist: {info['dist']:.4f} m")
        break

eval_env.close()

# Example 3: MuJoCo environment for a contact-rich pick task
# pip install mujoco gymnasium

import mujoco
import numpy as np
import gymnasium as gym
from gymnasium import spaces


class MuJoCoPickEnv(gym.Env):
    """Minimal pick-and-place using MuJoCo's Python bindings (mujoco>=3.0)."""

    MODEL_XML = """
    <mujoco>
      <option timestep="0.002"/>
      <worldbody>
        <light pos="0 0 3"/>
        <geom type="plane" size="1 1 0.1" rgba="0.8 0.8 0.8 1"/>
        <body name="object" pos="0.05 0 0.025">
          <joint type="free"/>
          <geom type="box" size="0.025 0.025 0.025" rgba="1 0.3 0.3 1"
                condim="6" friction="1 0.005 0.0001"/>
        </body>
      </worldbody>
    </mujoco>
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self.model = mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_string(self.MODEL_XML)
        self.data = mujoco.MjData(self.model)
        # Object: 3 pos + 4 quat + 3 vel + 3 ang_vel = 13
        self.observation_space = spaces.Box(-np.inf, np.inf, shape=(13,))
        # Toy: control object velocity directly (proxy for gripper)
        self.action_space = spaces.Box(-1.0, 1.0, shape=(3,))

    def _get_obs(self):
        obj_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(self.model, mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_BODY, "object")
        pos = self.data.xpos[obj_id].copy()
        quat = self.data.xquat[obj_id].copy()
        vel = self.data.cvel[obj_id, :3].copy()   # linear velocity
        ang = self.data.cvel[obj_id, 3:].copy()   # angular velocity
        return np.concatenate([pos, quat, vel, ang]).astype(np.float32)

    def step(self, action):
        obj_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(self.model, mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_BODY, "object")
        # Apply velocity impulse to object (simplified gripper proxy)
        self.data.xfrc_applied[obj_id, :3] = action * 5.0
        mujoco.mj_step(self.model, self.data)
        obs = self._get_obs()
        height = obs[2]  # z position
        reward = float(height > 0.1)   # reward for lifting
        terminated = height > 0.3
        truncated = self.data.time > 5.0
        return obs, reward, terminated, truncated, {}

    def reset(self, seed=None, options=None):
        mujoco.mj_resetData(self.model, self.data)
        return self._get_obs(), {}

project (2019) trained a five-fingered Shadow robot hand to solve a Rubik's Cube entirely in MuJoCo simulation, then transferred the policy to real hardware. The training required approximately 13,000 years of simulated experience, which was compressed to wall-clock days using thousands of parallel MuJoCo instances running on a distributed compute cluster. The key insight was that no single simulator run needed to be perfectly accurate — instead, domain randomization over thousands of varied simulation conditions produced a policy robust enough to handle the real robot's inevitable physics mismatches.

has more recently demonstrated training of humanoid locomotion policies in Isaac Sim at 100,000+ simulation steps per second on a single A100 GPU. The photorealistic rendering pipeline also enables training vision-based grasping policies directly from simulated RGB-D images, dramatically narrowing the visual domain gap compared to older non-photorealistic simulators.

A timestep that is too large (> 5 ms for stiff contacts) causes integration instability. A timestep that is too small wastes computation. Match the timestep to the stiffness of the contacts in the scene.

Commanding joint positions directly in simulation, when the real robot uses PID-controlled torques with finite bandwidth, creates a dynamics mismatch. Model actuator delays and velocity/torque limits.

Reward shaping that works beautifully in sim can incentivize exploiting physics artifacts. If the reward includes terms only measurable in simulation (ground-truth contact forces, internal state), the policy will overfit to the simulator.
Loading a URDF without verifying mass/inertia parameters against the real robot's spec sheet is a common source of large sim-to-real gaps. Measure or compute inertia tensors from CAD.
Using position control in sim when torque control is needed in real.
Many simulators default to ideal position control. If the real robot is torque-controlled, the transfer will fail unless the sim and real control modes match.
Before RL training, verify that the simulated robot replicates known real-hardware behaviors: check that joint limits, mass, and center-of-mass match the manufacturer spec.
Model joint PD controllers, velocity limits, gear ratios, and transmission delays. Without these, the learned policy will command physically impossible joint trajectories.
Wrapping your environment in a Gymnasium-compatible class ensures compatibility with all major RL libraries (Stable-Baselines3, CleanRL, RL4J, etc.) and simplifies swapping algorithms.
Apply domain randomization from day one.
Randomizing physics parameters during training produces more robust policies than training on fixed parameters. It does not significantly increase training cost.
Log simulation metrics separately from RL metrics.
Track simulation step time, environment reset time, and contact events. Slow resets (common with complex collision meshes) bottleneck training more than policy inference.

Simulation throughput scales primarily with the number of parallel environments. CPU-based simulators like PyBullet and MuJoCo can run 4–32 environments in parallel using Python multiprocessing or Stable-Baselines3's vectorized env wrappers. GPU-based simulators like Isaac Sim and IsaacGym can run 1,000–10,000 parallel environments on a single high-end GPU, with the entire physics integration, rendering, and reward computation running in GPU kernels.
~1,000–10,000 steps/sec per environment on a single CPU core. Vectorizing 8 envs with SubprocVecEnv gives roughly linear speedup.
~10,000–50,000 steps/sec per env, more for simple scenes. MuJoCo's XLA-compatible MJX backend allows GPU-batched simulation in JAX.
100,000–1,000,000+ aggregate steps/sec across 1000+ parallel environments on a single A100. Entire RL pipeline (sim + policy + update) fits on GPU.
Free, easy to install; no GPU required
High-accuracy contact simulation, standard in academic RL
Free since 2022; MJX adds JAX GPU backend
Requires NVIDIA GPU; steep learning curve
Best for ROS2 deployments; slower for RL
Wraps PyBullet, MuJoCo, Isaac; ensures lib compatibility
Reliable PyTorch RL implementations (SAC, PPO, TD3)
Works with any Gymnasium environment; good defaults
Bridge between Gazebo topics and real ROS2 hardware stack
Essential for deploying policies trained in Gazebo to real robots
Physics simulators in production robot learning pipelines carry a specific class of reliability concern:

. A simulation can produce numerically unstable states (NaN joint positions, exploding velocities) that the environment wrapper propagates as valid observations to the policy. Unlike software crashes, NaN propagation can silently corrupt a training run for millions of steps before the loss curve reveals the problem.
Validate observations for NaN/Inf at every step in debug mode.
Use an observation wrapper that raises an exception if any element is not finite before forwarding to the policy.
Set hard limits on joint positions and velocities.
Clip or reset when the robot reaches configurations that would be physically impossible or dangerous on real hardware.
Never trust URDF mass/inertia values blindly.
Many open-source robot URDFs contain incorrect or placeholder inertia tensors. Verify against manufacturer data sheets or compute from CAD geometry.
Log checksum of the robot model at training start.
If the URDF changes between runs, the comparison of trained models across runs is invalid. Record the model hash in training metadata.
Physics simulators make deep RL for robotics tractable by providing fast, safe, resetable environments; without them, real-hardware training is orders of magnitude too slow.
Choose the simulator based on the task: PyBullet for prototyping, MuJoCo for contact-rich manipulation research, Isaac Sim for GPU-parallel vision-based training, Gazebo for ROS2 deployment.
The reality gap — the mismatch between simulated and real physics — is unavoidable; domain randomization and accurate actuator modeling are the primary mitigations.
Wrap any simulator in a Gymnasium-compatible environment to stay compatible with the broader RL library ecosystem (Stable-Baselines3, CleanRL, IsaacLab).
Model actuator dynamics (PD controllers, gear limits, delays) in simulation, not just idealized joint control, or the transfer to real hardware will fail.
Validate observations for NaN propagation in debug builds; silent numerical divergence can corrupt entire training runs without obvious early warnings.
GPU-based simulators like Isaac Sim can run 1,000+ parallel environments on a single GPU, compressing days of training into hours for locomotion and manipulation tasks.
